test_text.title: Advice on City Activities for Family Visitors

audioscript.text: Part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a man asking a friend for advice on things to do in the city with a family of visitors.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: For you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Sandra, I seem to remember you had some family visitors staying with you recently.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: My brother and his family were here a couple of months ago.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Ok, good.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, I wanted to ask your advice.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I've got my cousin and her family visiting next month, and as I don't have kids, I've no idea where to take them.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right, what about accommodation?
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Are they going to stay with you in your flat?
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: No, thankfully, there wouldn't be room.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: My cousin wants me to recommend a hotel.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Do you know anywhere?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I do actually.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: I always recommend people stay at the Kings Hotel.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Where's that near?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's about 5 minutes' walk from Murray station, so nice and central.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: It's actually on George Street.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, I know.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I think they're on quite a tight budget, so how much roughly is it to stay there?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: If you book a family room, it's about £125 per night.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: My brother paid for two double rooms in the end, and I think that was around £95 for each room.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Oh, that's not so bad.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So how old are your cousin's kids?
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: 12 and 9.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So I want to organize some trips while they're here.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I was thinking of doing a bus tour of the city center, as none of them have been here before.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Those bus tours are quite expensive.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: I think it's better to do a walking tour.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: It gives you a much better feel for the city.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: There's one that starts from Carlton Square.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: It takes a couple of hours and doesn't cost that much.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'll look that up, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: If the weather's nice, one thing you could do is visit the old fort.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: You could get there by boat.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: The whole trip takes half a day.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: That's a really good idea.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'd like to do that myself.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And if the weather's bad, I was thinking they could go to the science museum.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: But maybe they could do that when I'm at work.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, don't forget it's closed on Mondays.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: They're here from Saturday for 4 nights, so Tuesday would be best I think.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And it won't be so crowded then.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Saturdays are terrible.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: I took my kids to the exhibition on old computers there, and it was far too crowded.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: I wanted to go back, but it's finished now.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: That's a shame.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: My cousin's kids would have enjoyed that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: There's another one starting soon on space, which looks really good too.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Ok, well I'll mention that to my cousin.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Have you thought about where to take them to eat?
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Well, I really like all the food stalls at Clacton Market.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: uh My cousin's vegetarian, I know it's one of the best places for that kind of food.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Definitely.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: And there'll be loads of choice for the kids too.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: You need to get there quite early though, at the weekend most of the stalls stop serving lunch at 2:30.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Good point, it's all going to need careful planning.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: My cousin said she'd love to take the kids to a show at the theater, but tickets are so expensive.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I know, but you can get some good deals if you book online with bargaintickets.com for the following day.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: On some seats, there's a 75% discount.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm, I must try and get some.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, there are lots of things you can do for free as well.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: No need to spend a fortune.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Like what?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: They're coming next month, right?
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Well check and see if it's the same weekend as the Roots Music Festival in Blakewell Gardens.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: R double O T S.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, check it out online.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: It's always a family friendly event, and there's no entry charge.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: That sounds perfect.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And if you're in Blakewell Gardens - climb Telegraph Hill - you'll be able to look right down on the port.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Everyone's always really impressed because it's so huge.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Oh, yeah.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I've been meaning to do that for ages.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I've heard the view's amazing.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, it's really worth the effort.
audioscript.speaker: SANDRA

audioscript.text: Well, that's given me loads of ideas.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Thanks so much...
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: UK Football Stadium Tour and Football History Introduction

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear a guide talking to members of the public who are visiting a football stadium in the UK.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning and welcome to City Football Club.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'd like to give you some useful information about your visit to the stadium today.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And then we'll start the tour of the areas of the stadium that are open to visitors.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I can see lots of children here today, so just to let mums and dads know a few things before we start.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The stadium has lots of stairs, and the players' tunnel is very dark.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Please don't let your children wander off on their own, even for a minute.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We don't want any accidents or anyone getting frightened.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Cameras are permitted everywhere, and you can take pictures of your child shooting a penalty.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Assistants are helping to organize this, and hopefully the queue won't be too long.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's very hot and sunny out on the pitch today.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You can get food and drink at the cafe, and I really recommend the healthy lunch boxes for children.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Also in the cafe, children are invited to do a football themed drawing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We'll pick the best one at the end of the afternoon, so don't forget to put your name and contact details on the back.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That way, if you've left the stadium before then, we'll send your prize, but sadly we can't return drawings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'd like to mention some features of the tour.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We'll start with the three-sixty cinema experience, which has been very popular over the years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And then I'll take you to the players' dressing rooms, before going outside to the seating area and the pitch.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I should say if you'd prefer your visit to be self-guided, please collect headphones from the reception, and then you can listen to the pre-recorded information at your own speed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We've only just introduced this feature.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And would appreciate your feedback.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We're thinking of offering tours in other languages in future, so if you have any thoughts on that, we'd welcome those too.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you plan to return another time, you might like to book one of our VIP tours.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We've only just started offering these.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And they can be booked online.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, the stadium you see today was built in 1989, as part of a three-year redevelopment project.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: While that project was going on the team had to play its matches at the ground of another club.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Apart from that, the club has been here on this site since 1870.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As some of you may know, that was the start of a really important decade in the history of football in this country.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, 1870 was also the year that football teams started to include a player whose role it was to guard the goal.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's hard to imagine what the game must have been like without someone in that position, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In 1872 and 73, many other clubs were established, both here and abroad, and the following year, in 1874, referees were allowed to send players off if they committed certain offenses, and also in that year, teams started having to swap ends at half-time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One fact I was interested to discover was that in early football games, the aim was for the scorer to get the ball between two flag posts, and later between sticks joined at the top with a piece of tape.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In 1875, that tape was replaced with the solid crossbar that we're familiar with today.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: 1877 saw the founding of further new clubs, and the history books tell us that in the same year, all the clubs decided to set a limit of 90 minutes for each match.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before that, it was a more casual arrangement, and this sometimes caused huge arguments.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And sometimes fights during matches, when one team called the end of the game, and the other team wanted to play on to try and score a winning goal.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By 1878 the number of teams in the Football League increased again.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In addition, referees started using whistles.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And electric lamps were installed on certain pitches.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This was a significant change, as games could then be played in the evenings all year round.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In 1880, clubs began to charge fans for admission to games, even though players were still amateurs, and had other proper jobs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's hard to imagine in the modern professional game, where top players earn significant sums of money from both playing and commercial activities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part two.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: If you plan to return another time, you might like to book one of our VIP tours.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We've only just started offering these.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And they can be booked online.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now, the stadium you see today was built in 1989, as part of a three-year redevelopment project.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: While that project was going on the team had to play its matches at the ground of another club.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Apart from that, the club has been here on this site since 1870.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As some of you may know, that was the start of a really important decade in the history of football in this country.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, 1870 was also the year that football teams started to include a player whose role it was to guard the goal.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's hard to imagine what the game must have been like without someone in that position, isn't it?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In 1872 and 73, many other clubs were established, both here and abroad, and the following year, in 1874, referees were allowed to send players off if they committed certain offenses, and also in that year, teams started having to swap ends at half-time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One fact I was interested to discover was that in early football games, the aim was for the scorer to get the ball between two flag posts, and later between sticks joined at the top with a piece of tape.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In 1875, that tape was replaced with the solid crossbar that we're familiar with today.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: 1877 saw the founding of further new clubs, and the history books tell us that in the same year, all the clubs decided to set a limit of 90 minutes for each match.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before that, it was a more casual arrangement, and this sometimes caused huge arguments.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And sometimes fights during matches, when one team called the end of the game, and the other team wanted to play on to try and score a winning goal.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: By 1878 the number of teams in the Football League increased again.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In addition, referees started using whistles.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And electric lamps were installed on certain pitches.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This was a significant change, as games could then be played in the evenings all year round.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In 1880, clubs began to charge fans for admission to games, even though players were still amateurs, and had other proper jobs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's hard to imagine in the modern professional game, where top players earn significant sums of money from both playing and commercial activities.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part two.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO things does the speaker say about visiting the football stadium with children?
options: Children can get their photo taken with a football player.
There is a competition for children today.
Parents must stay with their children at all times.
Children will need sunhats and drinks.
The café has a special offer on meals for children.

body.title: Which TWO features of the stadium tour are new this year?
options: VIP tour
360 cinema experience
audio guide
dressing room tour
tours in other languages

text: Which event in the history of football in the UK took place in each of the following years?
textReadable: Which event in the history of football in the UK took place in each of the following years?

list: the introduction of pay for the players
a change to the design of the goal
the first use of lights for matches
two changes to the rules of the game
the introduction of a fee for spectators
an agreement on the length of a game
items: 1870 <input>
1874 <input>
1875 <input>
1877 <input>
1878 <input>
1880 <input>

body.listTitle: Events in the history of football

test_text.title: Discussion on Teaching Handwriting to Children

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear two education students discussing an assignment on teaching handwriting to children.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: How are you getting on with the assignment on handwriting?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Not too bad.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: You know, I hadn't realized that children benefit in so many ways from learning to write.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's such an important skill, and yet most people think handwriting is less important than in the past, because people hardly ever write by hand these days.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And all the evidence suggests children should learn to write by hand before they learn to type, not least because it helps their memory.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's right, the physical act of writing helps children to remember letters.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That seems pretty obvious when you think about it.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Ha, what's less obvious is how it helps develop their concentration.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: They have to sit still and focus on one thing.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That aspect of handwriting had never occurred to me before.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm, same here.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm not sure I understand how it improves children's imagination though.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, there was that study which showed that primary age children generated more ideas when they were writing by hand than using a keyboard.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I would have guessed that would be the case.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm, yeah.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I've never associated spatial awareness with handwriting either.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I thought spatial awareness was more to do with knowing where you are in relation to objects or other people.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm, I thought that too.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: But good spatial awareness is essential for writing, because you have to space words correctly.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's not just fine motor skills that improve through writing, as I'd always assumed.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: hmm Handwriting is so much harder for children with dyspraxia, who have problems coordinating movement.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's good there are lots of things you can do in the classroom to help them.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: They need so much more support with letter formation.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You need to play lots of games to help them distinguish letter shapes.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Huh, it takes a lot of patience.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I like the idea of using one of those pens that lights up if you press too hard.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That seems like a really simple solution.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, absolutely.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm not sure there's much you can do about children with dyspraxia writing very slowly.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's more important to focus on accuracy, and as they get more confident.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I think they eventually speed up.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: One quite simple thing you can do is to use grid paper.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So they write each letter in a box, and that trains them to space the letters correctly.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Indeed.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's more important for legibility than trying to get them to write in a straight line.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: For some children, it might be better to teach them to write on a laptop, rather than by hand.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Like children with dyslexia, they often really struggle with handwriting, and some just give up.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yeah, it's not as frustrating for them if they get things wrong on a keyboard, they can be more willing to have a go, but I read that developing fluency isn't any faster.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Did you read that article on the benefits of teaching print rather than cursive handwriting, where the letters are joined up?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, in the past cursive writing was certainly considered more stylish and educated.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: But not anymore.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Teachers' attitudes have changed because it's been proved that cursive is more difficult to learn, especially for children with learning difficulties who find joining up letters really challenging.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I agree.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I was always worried that my poor handwriting affected my exam results, and now research shows that I was right to worry.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm sure a lot of students think it's unfair that they're being judged on their handwriting.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Not just their knowledge, marks are definitely affected if examiners can't read the script.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's why it's always been so important to teach children to write legibly.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Do you think the role of handwriting will change in the future?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: hmm I can't see that changing much.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Touch typing still isn't taught in most schools.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Which is a shame, but maybe that won't be necessary in the future, because people will also be able to write by hand on digital devices.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Anyway.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Teachers understand the value of handwriting.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's a basic life skill.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: True.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: However, the fact is that people are writing by hand less and less and relying on digital devices.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That does cause some problems.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: hmm You mean like notetaking?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: There are lots of apps for that, and for reading historical documents apparently.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: But my mom is shocked by my awful spelling and the fact that my punctuation is really inconsistent.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I think you can put that down to lack of practice.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Ha ha, I expect so.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Personally, I miss writing by hand.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I hardly ever write anything now.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I remember my grandparents had such beautiful handwriting, and it was so individual.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Nobody I know would be able to identify my handwriting now.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's a shame.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I know, I feel the same way.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I used to write a diary by hand.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And now I do that digitally.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It just seems less effort to do it that way.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: hmm So it's not just a problem.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part three.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO benefits for children of learning to write did both students find surprising?
options: improved fine motor skills
improved memory
improved concentration
improved imagination
improved spatial awareness

body.title: For children with dyspraxia, which TWO problems with handwriting do the students think are easiest to correct?
options: not spacing letters correctly
not writing in a straight line
applying too much pressure when writing
confusing letter shapes
writing very slowly

items.title: What does the woman say about using laptops to teach writing to children with dyslexia?
options: Children often lack motivation to learn that way.
Children become fluent relatively quickly.
Children react more positively if they make a mistake.

items.title: When discussing whether to teach cursive or print writing, the woman thinks that
options: cursive writing disadvantages a certain group of children.
print writing is associated with lower academic performance.
most teachers in the UK prefer a traditional approach to handwriting.

items.title: According to the students, what impact does poor handwriting have on exam performance?
options: There is evidence to suggest grades are affected by poor handwriting.
Neat handwriting is less important now than it used to be.
Candidates write more slowly and produce shorter answers.

items.title: What prediction does the man make about the future of handwriting?
options: Touch typing will be taught before writing by hand.
Children will continue to learn to write by hand.
People will dislike handwriting on digital devices.

items.title: The woman is concerned that relying on digital devices has made it difficult for her to
options: take detailed notes.
spell and punctuate.
read old documents.

items.title: How do the students feel about their own handwriting?
options: concerned they are unable to write quickly
embarrassed by comments made about it
regretful that they have lost the habit

test_text.title: The Impact of Birds of Prey on a Zambian Farming Community

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear part of an environmental science lecture about the impact of birds of prey on a farming community in Africa.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: We've been looking at different types of conflicts that may arise between wildlife and humans at the boundaries of protected areas, such as national parks and animal sanctuaries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'd like to illustrate this by telling you about some research that I've been involved in recently, in the Central African country of Zambia, in the area around the Chembe Bird Sanctuary.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which contains over 300 of the listed birds of Zambia.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These include a number of birds of prey such as eagles, hawks and owls that live by hunting and killing other birds and animals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, most of the people living in the local communities near to the bird sanctuary are small scale farmers, and these birds of prey provide important social and ecological benefits to them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, a lot of damage can be caused to farmers' crops by rodents such as rats, which would consume the crops as they grow in the fields, as well as after harvesting, if they weren't hunted and killed by the birds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the predatory habits of these birds also protect farmers in other ways.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: uh For example, a major danger to rural workers is snakes, whose bite may be dangerous or even fatal.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And birds of prey have a major role in keeping their populations under control.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Local people have always been aware of these benefits, and for years even before the sanctuary was opened in 1973, the birds played a key role in the culture of the region.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, more recently the sanctuary and its birds have also become increasingly important to the community in economic terms, because at present, after a relatively slow start.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Tourism has become an important source of revenue for them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, although these birds of prey are protected by the government, their numbers are falling.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some of these deaths are accidental.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Fatalities occur when birds alight on roads to catch and eat their prey, and are hit by fast-moving traffic.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Drivers in Zambia have to take special care at night, as birds may regard the quieter roads as safe places to sleep.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Accidental deaths may also occur if these birds fly close to high voltage power lines, as they may be electrocuted.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is a particular danger in the heavy rain which can occur in the region in the months from December to April.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And local farmers also pose a threat to these birds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As well as growing crops, small scale farmers in the area also rear chickens.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These provide food for the farmer's families, as well as being an important source of income.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But they're also an easy target for birds of prey.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And so farmers may shoot these birds, which is illegal but understandable, or they may poison the birds, which again is illegal and can have negative effects on the ecosystem.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So how else can farmers protect their chickens from birds of prey?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some people believe that to prevent the predators from settling near the area where the chickens are kept.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's best to keep this area free from vegetation, but in fact this is counterproductive, as it means the chickens have no cover to hide in.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And they'll be easier for the birds to see.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another possibility would be to prevent the chickens from going outside at all, and to keep them safe from predators inside a building.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But this would cost far too much to be a practical solution.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Nearly all the farmers reported that they spent a lot of time and effort trying to frighten off the birds of prey without actually harming them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Most of the farmers had at least one dog, and said this was a big help at scaring away the predators.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some of the farmers also reported that during the breeding season.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When the chickens were particularly vulnerable, they encouraged their children to watch over the chickens, and to hit pans with a metal spoon, so that the resulting noise would succeed in driving away birds that were trying to seize the young chicks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: None of these methods was 100% effective, so as a result the village people told us that rather than just using one method.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They were forced to use a combination for them to have any effect. And even so, these birds of prey remain a major threat to the chickens' survival and cause considerable economic loss to farmers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So we looked at the possibility of a longer-term solution to...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 4, you will hear part of an environmental science lecture about the impact of birds of prey on a farming community in Africa.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: We've been looking at different types of conflicts that may arise between wildlife and humans at the boundaries of protected areas, such as national parks and animal sanctuaries.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I'd like to illustrate this by telling you about some research that I've been involved in recently, in the Central African country of Zambia, in the area around the Chembe Bird Sanctuary.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Which contains over 300 of the listed birds of Zambia.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These include a number of birds of prey such as eagles, hawks and owls that live by hunting and killing other birds and animals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, most of the people living in the local communities near to the bird sanctuary are small scale farmers, and these birds of prey provide important social and ecological benefits to them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, a lot of damage can be caused to farmers' crops by rodents such as rats, which would consume the crops as they grow in the fields, as well as after harvesting, if they weren't hunted and killed by the birds.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And the predatory habits of these birds also protect farmers in other ways.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: uh For example, a major danger to rural workers is snakes, whose bite may be dangerous or even fatal.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And birds of prey have a major role in keeping their populations under control.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Local people have always been aware of these benefits, and for years even before the sanctuary was opened in 1973, the birds played a key role in the culture of the region.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, more recently the sanctuary and its birds have also become increasingly important to the community in economic terms, because at present, after a relatively slow start.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Tourism has become an important source of revenue for them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, although these birds of prey are protected by the government, their numbers are falling.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some of these deaths are accidental.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Fatalities occur when birds alight on roads to catch and eat their prey, and are hit by fast-moving traffic.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Drivers in Zambia have to take special care at night, as birds may regard the quieter roads as safe places to sleep.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Accidental deaths may also occur if these birds fly close to high voltage power lines, as they may be electrocuted.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is a particular danger in the heavy rain which can occur in the region in the months from December to April.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And local farmers also pose a threat to these birds.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As well as growing crops, small scale farmers in the area also rear chickens.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These provide food for the farmer's families, as well as being an important source of income.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But they're also an easy target for birds of prey.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And so farmers may shoot these birds, which is illegal but understandable, or they may poison the birds, which again is illegal and can have negative effects on the ecosystem.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So how else can farmers protect their chickens from birds of prey?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some people believe that to prevent the predators from settling near the area where the chickens are kept.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's best to keep this area free from vegetation, but in fact this is counterproductive, as it means the chickens have no cover to hide in.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And they'll be easier for the birds to see.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another possibility would be to prevent the chickens from going outside at all, and to keep them safe from predators inside a building.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But this would cost far too much to be a practical solution.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Nearly all the farmers reported that they spent a lot of time and effort trying to frighten off the birds of prey without actually harming them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Most of the farmers had at least one dog, and said this was a big help at scaring away the predators.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some of the farmers also reported that during the breeding season.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When the chickens were particularly vulnerable, they encouraged their children to watch over the chickens, and to hit pans with a metal spoon, so that the resulting noise would succeed in driving away birds that were trying to seize the young chicks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: None of these methods was 100% effective, so as a result the village people told us that rather than just using one method.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They were forced to use a combination for them to have any effect. And even so, these birds of prey remain a major threat to the chickens' survival and cause considerable economic loss to farmers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So we looked at the possibility of a longer-term solution to...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Research in the area around the Chembe Bird Sanctuary
